contract.title: Will the US enter a recession in 2026?
contract.resolution_criteria: Resolves YES if the National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER) Business Cycle Dating Committee announces that the United States entered an economic recession with a peak month in 2026, on or before 2026-12-31. Resolves NO otherwise.

conditions.label: NBER 2026-peak recession by 2026-12-31
conditions.summary: The NBER Business Cycle Dating Committee publicly announces that the U.S. entered a recession with the peak month occurring in 2026, and this announcement is made on or before 2026-12-31.

contract.narrative: Regulatory signals are mixed: late-2025 capital rule changes and 2026 risk-weighted asset rules tighten conditions, while CBLR easing and small fiscal measures (child deposits, tax-cut messaging) temper credit stress. OCC/FDIC enforcement actions and a new ratings-system review point to ongoing scrutiny, nudging odds up slightly. NBER’s 2026 recession call remains pending as banks adapt to capital rules and incoming data.

timeline.title: OCC Announces Enforcement Actions for May 2026
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
timeline.one_line_why: OCC enforcement actions against several banks signal limited banking stress, slightly increasing recession odds but not directly tied to NBER dating.

timeline.title: Agencies Request Comment on Financial Institutions Rating System - 05/19/2026
timeline.regulator: Federal Financial Institutions Examination Council
timeline.one_line_why: Interagency request on financial-institution ratings signals regulatory scrutiny of banking health, marginally increasing recession likelihood but is indirect.

timeline.title: Economic Policy Statements to TBAC: 2026 - 2nd Quarter​
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury
timeline.one_line_why: Treasury TBAC policy statements include official economic outlooks that slightly shift recession odds but do not directly determine an NBER 2026 peak.

timeline.title: Apr 23, 2026 - Final Rule on Revisions to the Community Bank Leverage Ratio (CBLR) Framework | FIL-19-2026
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: FDIC CBLR revisions modestly ease community bank capital constraints, slightly boosting lending and marginally reducing near‑term recession risk (low impact).

timeline.title: April 23, 2026 - Notational Vote: Final Regulatory Capital Rule
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: Final bank capital rule affects banks' lending/resilience, giving a small ambiguous impact on recession odds but doesn't directly determine an NBER 2026 peak.

timeline.title: Agencies Finalize Changes to Community Bank Leverage Ratio
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: Finalized changes to the community bank leverage ratio affect small‑bank capital and credit supply—small, indirect signal on recession risk, not decisive.

timeline.title: Agencies Finalize Changes to Community Bank Leverage Ratio
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
timeline.one_line_why: Finalized changes to community bank leverage rules slightly affect banking stability and credit supply—an indirect, small influence on recession odds in 2026.

timeline.title: Community Bank Leverage Ratio: Final Rule
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
timeline.one_line_why: OCC final rule on community bank leverage affects small banks' capital and credit supply—an indirect, limited influence on recession odds for 2026.

timeline.title: Treasury International Capital Data for February
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury — Office of Public Affairs
timeline.one_line_why: Monthly TIC capital‑flow release is peripheral financial data that offers only minor information about stress/flows and thus only slightly impacts recession odds.

timeline.title: Consolidated Reports of Condition and Income for First Quarter 2026
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation — Division of Risk Management Supervision
timeline.one_line_why: Routine release of Q1 Call Reports provides banking-sector data that may inform recession risk but doesn't directly determine NBER dating.

timeline.title: Regulatory Capital Rule: Regulatory Capital and Standardized Approach for Risk-weighted Assets
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: New standardized risk-weighted capital rule likely tightens bank capital, marginally reducing lending and slightly increasing near-term recession probability.

timeline.title: Regulatory Capital Rule: Category I and II Banking Organizations, Banking Organizations with Significant Trading Activity, and Optional Adoption for Other Banking Organizations
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: Capital-rule changes affect bank lending and stability and thus recession risk indirectly, but are ambiguous and not determinative of an NBER 2026 peak.

timeline.title: Regulatory Capital: Standardized Approach for Risk-Weighted Assets
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
timeline.one_line_why: OCC capital-rule changes can modestly affect bank lending and macro risk, but are indirect and unlikely to determine NBER dating.

timeline.title: Treasury International Capital Data for January
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury — Office of Public Affairs
timeline.one_line_why: Routine Treasury TIC January release is an indirect capital-flow datapoint that offers only a minor, non-decisive signal for NBER recession dating.

timeline.title: President Trump’s Tax Cuts are Putting More Money Back into the Pockets of American Families and Workers
timeline.regulator: U.S. Department of the Treasury
timeline.one_line_why: Treasury press release touting tax-cut stimulus slightly reduces near-term recession odds, modestly lowering chance NBER finds a 2026 peak.

timeline.title: Treasury, IRS issue proposed regulations for Trump Accounts pilot program, Treasury Department to deposit $1,000 into the account of each eligible child
timeline.regulator: Department of the Treasury and Internal Revenue Service
timeline.one_line_why: A modest $1,000 child deposit is small fiscal stimulus that slightly reduces odds of a 2026 NBER recession (makes YES less likely).

timeline.title: Consolidated Reports of Condition and Income for Fourth Quarter 2025
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation — Division of Risk Management Supervision
timeline.one_line_why: Routine Q4 2025 bank condition reports provide incremental signals about financial stress but don't directly determine NBER recession dating.

timeline.title: Regulatory Capital Rule: Modifications to the Enhanced Supplementary Leverage Ratio Standards for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies and Their Subsidiary Depository Institutions; Total Loss-Absorbing Capacity and Long-Term Debt Requirements for U.S. Global Systemically Important Bank Holding Companies 12 CFR Part 324 RIN 3064–AG11 Final rule Effective Date: January 1, 2026
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency; Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System; Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: Jan 1, 2026 GSIB enhanced SLR/TLAC rule changes could tighten lending and financial conditions, modestly raising the chance of a 2026 NBER recession.

timeline.title: Agencies Issue Final Rule to Modify Certain Regulatory Capital Standards
timeline.regulator: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency
timeline.one_line_why: Changes to bank capital rules can modestly affect credit supply and thus 2026 recession odds, but the rule’s direction and magnitude for growth are unclear.

timeline.title: FDIC Announces Three Orders Against Discover Bank, Greenwood, Delaware
timeline.regulator: Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation
timeline.one_line_why: FDIC enforcement against Discover signals some banking-sector stress that slightly raises the chance of a 2026 NBER recession, but is indirect and limited.

primary_drivers: Final Community Bank Leverage Ratio and regulatory capital rules (Apr 23)
Interagency request for comment on financial institutions rating system (May 19)
heat_panel.explainer: This week’s critical heat is driven by late‑April finalization of community bank leverage and capital rules, plus recent OCC enforcement actions and an interagency request for comment.
